subtopic: Snow Leopard, Rhino, and Nilgiri Tahr
title: Snow Leopard
focus: Habitat, characteristics, conservation status, and international/national conservation efforts for the Snow Leopard.
rationale: The Snow Leopard is a highly examinable species due to its specific high-altitude habitat, unique characteristics, and multiple international conservation agreements (Bishkek, Samarkand).
sectionExcerpt: Study estimated 477 snow leopards in Ladakh. Habitat: Mountainous regions of South and Central Asia. India: Jammu & Kashmir, Ladakh, Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Sikkim and Arunachal Pradesh. Globally, Hemis National Park, Kargil, and Leh have highest extensive densities. Characteristics: Solitary animals; unlike other big cats they cannot roar. Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I; CITES: Appendix I; CMS: Appendix I; IUCN Status: VU (Vulnerable). Conservation Efforts: Project Snow Leopard India; International Big Cat Alliance; Global Snow Leopard and Ecosystem Protection Programme; Bishkek Declaration (2017); Samarkand Resolution (2024); CaTRAT to identify Himalayan snow leopards.
evidenceBullets: IUCN Status is Vulnerable (VU); listed under WPA Schedule I, CITES Appendix I, and CMS Appendix I.
Found in mountainous regions of J&K, Ladakh, HP, Uttarakhand, Sikkim, and Arunachal Pradesh.
Hemis National Park, Kargil, and Leh have the highest extensive densities globally.
Unlike other big cats, snow leopards cannot roar.
Conservation initiatives include the Bishkek Declaration (2017), Samarkand Resolution (2024), and the CaTRAT (Camera Trap data Repository and Analysis Tool).

subtopic: Snow Leopard, Rhino, and Nilgiri Tahr
title: Greater One-Horned Rhino (Indian Rhino)
focus: DNA profiling, habitat distribution, species comparison, and conservation initiatives of the Indian Rhino.
rationale: The Indian Rhino is a critical megafauna species. UPSC frequently tests species comparisons (the 5 rhino species and their IUCN statuses) and specific habitats (Kaziranga).
sectionExcerpt: Assam Forest Department started DNA profiling of Rhino horns and add it to Rhino DNA Index System (RhoDIS) India DNA data library. Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I; CITES: Appendix I; IUCN Status: VU (Vulnerable). Habitat: Assam has the world's largest population. More than 90% of them are in Kaziranga National Park. Characteristics: One among 5 species of rhino: 2 African: White Rhino (Near Threatened), Black Rhino (Critically Endangered). 3 Asian: Sumatran (Critically Endangered), Javan (Critically Endangered), and Indian Rhino. Indian Rhino is the largest of all Asian rhino species. Usually solitary. Conservation initiatives: National Rhino Conservation Strategy 2019; New Delhi Declaration on Asian Rhinos 2019; Indian Rhino Vision 2020.
evidenceBullets: IUCN Status of Indian Rhino is Vulnerable (VU); listed under WPA Schedule I and CITES Appendix I.
Assam holds the world's largest population, with over 90% located in Kaziranga National Park.
Indian Rhino is the largest of all Asian rhino species.
Other rhino species statuses: White (Near Threatened), Black (Critically Endangered), Sumatran (Critically Endangered), Javan (Critically Endangered).
RhoDIS India is the DNA data library used for profiling rhino horns.

subtopic: Snow Leopard, Rhino, and Nilgiri Tahr
title: Nilgiri Tahr
focus: Habitat, endemism, cultural significance, and conservation status of the Nilgiri Tahr.
rationale: The Nilgiri Tahr is important due to its endemism to the Western Ghats, its status as the only mountain ungulate in southern India, and its unique mention in ancient Sangam literature.
sectionExcerpt: Traditional Nilgiri tahr habitats in Coimbatore Forest Division show signs of population revival. Habitat: Open montane grassland habitats. Endemic to Western Ghats. Eravikulam National Park, Kerala has the largest contiguous population. Characteristics: Only mountain ungulate in southern India. Locally known as Varaiaadu. Silappathikaram and Sivakasindamani mention its descriptions. Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I; IUCN Status: Endangered (EN). Conservation Efforts: Project Nilgiri Tahr of Tamil Nadu. State animal of Tamil Nadu. Nilgiri Tahr Day (October 07).
evidenceBullets: Endemic to the open montane grassland habitats of the Western Ghats.
Eravikulam National Park in Kerala hosts the largest contiguous population.
It is the only mountain ungulate in southern India.
Mentioned in ancient Tamil literature such as Silappathikaram and Sivakasindamani.
IUCN Status is Endangered (EN); it is the State animal of Tamil Nadu.
